title: Hand-cut tabs and dividers
subtitle: Measuring, cutting, and fitting tabbed divider pages in a journal or notebook
excerpt: A tabbed divider separates sections and lets the reader open directly to the right part of a journal. Hand-cutting tabs from card stock costs nothing and can be fitted to any existing notebook. The key measurements are the tab width, the step between tabs, and the setback from the fore-edge so tabs do not get caught when the journal is closed.
sourceNotes: Hand-cut tab dividers are a long-established stationery craft technique. No single pre-1928 primary source covers the contemporary journal application. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The distance between the outer edge of the tab and the fore-edge of the journal page, measured so the tab clears the cover when the journal is closed.
The distance each successive tab is offset toward the head or tail of the page, so a row of tabs is visible simultaneously when the journal is closed.

content.text: A well-cut tab sits flush with the fore-edge of the closed journal and does not catch on the cover. The critical measurement is the
tab setback
: cut the tab so it protrudes 5 to 8 mm beyond the page edge, no more. Wider tabs catch and tear.
Keep fingers behind the blade path when cutting along a ruler. Score the cut line with light pressure two or three times rather than forcing the knife through in a single heavy stroke.
What you need
Card stock for dividers: 130 to 180 gsm is stiff enough to stand up to repeated use without buckling. Lighter paper tears at the tab root after a few openings.
Measure the journal page dimensions first. The divider sheet is cut to the same height and width as the journal pages; the tab is an additional protrusion cut from the same sheet.
Calculating the tab positions
Decide how many sections the journal needs. Three to six sections is a practical maximum for a standard A5 journal.
Divide the page height by the number of sections to get the
tab step
. For a 210 mm tall A5 page with six sections: 210 ÷ 6 = 35 mm per step.
Mark the tab position on each divider sheet in pencil: tab 1 sits at the head, tab 2 is 35 mm lower, and so on.
Tab width should be 20 to 30 mm. Narrower tabs are harder to grab; wider tabs overlap the tab step and hide one another.
Cutting the tabs
Mark the tab rectangle on the right-hand edge of the divider sheet. The tab protrudes 6 mm to the right of the page edge.
Cut the top and bottom horizontal edges of the tab with the craft knife and ruler. Then cut the vertical outer edge of the tab.
Cut the recesses above and below the tab: these are the rectangular notches cut from the page edge on either side, leaving the tab as the only protrusion.
Round the outer corners of the tab with scissors to prevent corner-catching.
Fitting dividers into an existing journal

If inserting dividers into a sewn journal, cut each divider sheet to exactly the bound page size before tabbing: the binding does not allow pages wider than the text block. For a loose-leaf or disc-bound journal, punch holes to match the journal's spine system before cutting the tabs.
